title: Chicken goujons
subtitle: Breadcrumbed chicken strips with a sharp dipping sauce
excerpt: Chicken breast cut into strips, coated in seasoned breadcrumbs, and shallow-fried until golden and crisp. The definitive homemade version of the pub and school staple.
sourceNotes: Chicken goujons are a standard of British pub and casual restaurant cooking from the late twentieth century. The dish is a British adaptation of the French goujon (a breadcrumbed fish strip), applied to chicken breast. The technique follows the standard British breadcrumbing method.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze after breadcrumbing, before cooking. Cook from frozen at 200°C fan for 20 to 25 minutes.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles easily. Make a large batch and freeze in portions.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Breadcrumb up to 4 hours ahead and refrigerate uncovered. Cooking must be done fresh.

content.text: Homemade goujons are better than the shop-bought version in every respect: the crumb is crisper, the chicken is thicker and juicier, and they taste of seasoning rather than stabilisers. The key is the standard breadcrumbing process: flour first to dry the surface, egg to adhere, breadcrumbs to coat. Press the crumb on firmly so it sticks through cooking.

Panko breadcrumbs give a better, lighter crunch than standard dried breadcrumbs. Fresh breadcrumbs made from yesterday's white bread also work well and produce a different texture: denser and more substantial. Use whichever you prefer. The oil should be at 170°C to 180°C for shallow-frying; test with a breadcrumb before the goujons go in.

items.prepNote: cut into strips about 2 cm wide

content.text: Mix the panko breadcrumbs with {{smoked-paprika}} of smoked paprika and garlic powder if using. Set up three shallow bowls: seasoned flour, beaten eggs, seasoned panko. Working in batches, coat each {{chicken-breast}} chicken strip in flour (tapping off the excess), then egg (letting the excess drip off), then the panko, pressing firmly to adhere the crumb all over. Place on a plate while you coat the rest.

Pour sunflower oil to a depth of 1 cm in a wide frying pan. Heat over medium-high heat until a breadcrumb dropped in sizzles immediately. Fry the goujons in batches for 3 minutes per side until deep golden and cooked through. Do not crowd the pan. Drain on kitchen paper and keep warm.
Mix together the mayonnaise, Dijon mustard, and lemon juice for the dipping sauce. Season and serve alongside the goujons with lemon wedges.

items.symptom: Breadcrumb is falling off during cooking
items.cause: Crumb was not pressed on firmly, or the flour layer was too thick
items.fix: Press the crumb onto each strip firmly and let it sit for a few minutes before cooking. Ensure the flour layer is a light dusting, not a thick coat.

items.symptom: Goujons are golden on the outside but raw inside
items.cause: Oil was too hot, cooking the crumb before the chicken was cooked through
items.fix: Reduce the heat slightly and test a small piece first. The oil should sizzle actively but not smoke. For thick strips, finish in a 180°C oven for 5 minutes after frying.

items.cause: Oil was not hot enough when the goujons went in
items.fix: The oil must be at about 170°C before frying. Test with a dropped crumb: it should sizzle immediately and colour within 30 seconds.

items.cause: Strips were too thin, or overcooked
items.fix: Cut strips at least 2 cm wide. They only need 3 minutes per side; beyond that, the breast will dry out.

content.text: Chicken goujons became a fixture of British pub menus and children's menus in the 1980s and 1990s, when casual dining expanded and chicken breast became the most commercially dominant cut. The name is French (a goujon is originally a thin breadcrumbed strip of fish, usually sole), but the British chicken version became a dish in its own right. Homemade goujons made with proper chicken breast and panko are substantially better than the frozen industrial variety, which are partly responsible for the dish's reputation as a children's food rather than something worth eating as an adult.
